summary: Cathy O'Neil argues that Facebook's engagement-optimizing algorithm has become a major threat to democracy by spreading fake news and creating ideological echo chambers. She notes that because Facebook optimizes for time-on-site rather than truth, misinformation has proliferated unchecked. The post also criticizes media obsession with polling data at the expense of substantive policy discussion. O'Neil calls for Facebook to hire editors to curate content, redesign its algorithm to burst filter bubbles, and for media to reduce poll coverage in favor of real issues.
key_points: Facebook's algorithm optimizes for engagement, not truth, causing misinformation to 'balloon beyond recognition'
Hyper-partisan pseudo-news sites have emerged specifically to game Facebook's algorithm
The decline of traditional journalism is partly caused by ad dollars flowing to Facebook instead
Filter bubbles push people into ideological corners and make them view political opponents as 'bad people'
Media obsession with polling crowds out substantive policy discussion
money_quotes: When you optimize to something, and when you ignore something else, that other thing can be expected to balloon beyond recognition.
In this example, the thing Facebook has ignored is 'truth.'
This is bad for democracy, because people are constantly being misinformed or hysterically informed.
It's pushing people further into their corners, or pushing them off of Facebook and politics entirely.
We've ended up thinking people who don't agree with us are actually bad people.
This will take an army of workers, but there are plenty of journalists who are looking for jobs, and Facebook makes tons of money, so there's no actual problem besides the will of Facebook.
key_insight: When platforms optimize for engagement while ignoring truth, misinformation isn't a bug—it's the predictable consequence of the system working exactly as designed.
